readme.title: Literature review, published findings recomputed on Israeli data
readme.purpose: Each paper reports findings about startups and founders. For every finding we recompute the same statistic on Israeli ecosystem data from our own database and place the two side by side. Paper numbers come from the cited tables. Israeli numbers are computed offline from the database and are never invented.
howToNavigate: papers is the list of papers. Each new paper is appended here.

Within a paper, claims is the list of comparable statistics. For a single statistic paper like Azoulay there is one claim repeated across populations. For a multi statistic paper like Ewens each claim is a different statistic.
Each claim carries the paper value and our value, a unit, the source table, methodology and takeaway text in explanations, and detailRows.

detailRows holds the paper row and one or more Israeli rows. The Israeli row labelled Same paper years is the headline comparison. Other Israeli rows are alternative cohorts shown in the dropdown, for example a stricter venture definition or a wider year window. An Israeli row may also carry a compareTo block holding the paper figure for that same subgroup, for example a sector, a gender or a patent subset. Those paired rows render in the dropdown under Matched subgroups, while the plain Israeli slices sit under More Israeli cohorts above them.
comparisonLayout on the paper says how column three renders. shared means one common axis for every claim. perStat means each claim is its own statistic in its own unit.
conventions: All displayed text avoids colons and em-dashes by house style.

Israeli founding age is read from registry founding year. When a paper measures age at a later moment, such as first employer or first financing, we shift our figure forward to that moment and report it as the adjusted value.
For skewed money and ratio statistics the median is the more comparable figure than the mean, because a few very large values pull the mean up. Both are provided.

Where a paper reports the distribution we draw a horizontal box plot. The box is the middle half of the data, the 25th to the 75th percentile, when quartiles are given, otherwise one standard deviation either side of the mean. The whiskers run to the minimum and maximum, with the mean tick and the median where given. For skewed money and calendar year statistics the upper whisker is capped at the standard box plot fence, the 75th percentile plus one and a half times the middle half, so a single very large value does not flatten the axis. Counts such as financing rounds keep their true maximum. Where a paper reports no distribution we compare only the mean, median and standard deviation and show no whiskers.
Israeli numbers exclude implausible founder ages outside 18 to 80 and use real dates when both founding and round dates are present.

Gap size tells you whether a gap is small or large relative to the standard deviation of the statistic. For a continuous statistic we scale the gap by the pooled standard deviation of the two sides. For a share we use the standard deviation of a proportion, the square root of p times one minus p, since a share has a known standard deviation even when none is reported. When the paper publishes no standard deviation for a ratio, we scale the gap by the Israeli standard deviation and treat the size as approximate.

unitGlossary.meaning: A number of years, for example a founder age or a firm age.
A percentage from 0 to 100, for example a failure rate.
A multiple, for example a value step up of 1.6 times.
A headcount with one decimal, for example an average founding team size.
A count of financing rounds with one decimal, for example an average of 3.5 rounds.
A calendar year, for example a median founding year of 2005.

papers.title: Age and High-Growth Entrepreneurship
papers.authorsFull: Pierre Azoulay, Benjamin F. Jones, J. Daniel Kim, Javier Miranda
papers.comparisonNote: Every figure here comes from the paper Table 1, with the exited companies row from Table 2. Both report a mean founder age, a standard deviation and a count for each population. We recompute each figure on the matching Israeli companies.
papers.abstract: Many observers, and many investors, believe that young people are especially likely to produce the most successful new firms. Integrating administrative data on firms, workers, and owners, we study start-ups systematically in the United States and find successful entrepreneurs are middle-aged, not young. The mean age at founding for the 1-in-1,000 fastest growing new ventures is 45.0. The findings are similar when considering high-technology sectors, entrepreneurial hubs, and firm exits. Prior experience in the specific industry predicts much greater rates of entrepreneurial success. These findings strongly reject common hypotheses that emphasize youth as a key trait of entrepreneurs.
keyFindings: Mean founder age at the fastest growing ventures, the top 0.1 percent by employment five years after founding, is 45.0.
The same middle aged pattern appears in high technology sectors and in entrepreneurial hubs.
Founders behind successful exits, defined as any acquisition or public listing, are older still, with a mean near 47.
Prior experience in the specific industry predicts success far more than youth.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States companies that have employees. It treats a company as born in the first year it reports having staff, which can be later than the year it was legally registered.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli startup founders with a non null estimated age and no experience or minimum age cutoff
claims.source: Table 1 - United States entire, all start-ups

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper measures a founder age in the year their company first appears as an employer in United States Census data, meaning the first year it carries people on payroll. This first employer year typically falls after the legal incorporation date, because a company with no employees yet does not appear in those records.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We build each company timeline from public company registry records and use the registered founding date as the reference point. For companies that were never formally registered we estimate the founding date from other public signals, such as the earliest point they became visible in the public record.

explanations.title: Adjustment shown
explanations.body: We shift the Israeli founding age toward the first employer timing the paper uses. A conservative lower bound adds two years, the same gap the paper itself tested and found did not change its result. An upper bound uses the typical delay we observe between founding and first venture funding. We report the midpoint of that window, the average of the two bounds, as the Israeli figure.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The Israeli founding age sits a little earlier in company life than the employer date the paper uses. After we adjust it forward to the paper timing, the broad Israeli founder is within about 2.4 years of the United States figure of 41.9. That distance is small relative to the standard deviation of founder ages, so the pattern of middle aged founders looks similar in Israel.

intro: The paper counts founder age in the first year a company reports employees, while our records use the founding date, which is usually earlier. For each population we therefore shift the Israeli founding age forward to the paper timing before comparing.

Across the broad all employers group the typical Israeli founder is within about 2.4 years of the United States figure. The gap stays small for venture backed and patenting companies, at 2.2 years and 3.3 years. Exited companies are the exception at 9.1 years. Unlike the other cohorts this gap holds across venture backed and above 100 million dollar sub groups, pointing to a genuine ecosystem difference rather than a measurement error.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli startups with a venture backed round that is not equity excluded

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: This group is companies that raised venture capital at some point. The founder age is still measured in the first year the company reported having staff, so the group is venture backed while the timing is the first employer year.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: This group is Israeli startups that raised a venture round. We read the founding date from public company registry records and show an adjusted age that shifts it forward to match the paper timing. For these companies the date of the first venture round is a useful extra check, since it moves in the same direction as the first employer year the paper uses.

explanations.title: Adjustment shown
explanations.body: The same two year lower bound applies, because the paper checks that large timing gaps do not drive the result. The upper bound matters most here, since for venture backed companies we can watch the real delay from founding to the first venture round. For Israeli venture backed startups founded between 2007 and 2014 a founding age of 36.8 becomes a range of 38.8 to 40.6, and we report the midpoint of 39.7 as the Israeli figure.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Venture backed here means a company that raised at least one venture round, defined as a priced equity investment from an institutional venture capital fund. After shifting the Israeli founding age forward to the paper timing it averages 39.7, about 2.2 years below the United States figure of 41.9. That is a small gap relative to the standard deviation of founder ages, so once the timing difference is accounted for the middle aged founding pattern the paper documents holds for the Israeli venture backed cohort.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli startups linked to patents

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: This group is companies that were granted at least one patent. The founder age is measured in the first year the company reported having staff, the same as everywhere else in the paper.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: This group is Israeli startups linked to patents. We read the founding date from public company registry records and show an adjusted age that shifts it forward to match the paper timing.

explanations.title: Adjustment shown
explanations.body: For Israeli patenting startups founded between 2007 and 2014 the founding age is 38.2. Adding the two year employer gap gives 40.2, and using the observed delay from founding to first venture funding for this group gives 42.4.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Patenting here means a company granted at least one patent. These founders are the oldest group in both countries, which already matches the paper. After the timing adjustment the Israeli age is 41.3, a gap of 3.3 years below the United States figure of 44.6. Measured against the standard deviation of founder ages this is a medium sized gap, so Israeli patenting founders sit close to but a little younger than their United States peers.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli startups recorded as acquired or as having gone public

detailRows.label: Above $100M
detailRows.population: Exited startups with known deal value above $100M

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: An exit here means the company was acquired or went public. The paper uses companies founded between 2007 and 2009, because it needs at least five years of history to see whether each one reached that outcome.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: An exit on our side is a startup recorded as acquired or as having gone public. We read the founding date from public company registry records and show an adjusted age that shifts it forward to match the paper timing. Two sub-cohorts appear in the dropdown. VC-Backed Exits includes companies that raised at least one VC round before exiting. Above $100M includes companies whose acquisition price or IPO valuation is on record above that level, covering roughly 40 percent of our acquisitions since the rest have undisclosed deal terms.

explanations.title: Adjustment shown
explanations.body: The same timing logic applies, with two years as the employer gap and the delay from founding to first venture funding as the larger maturity proxy. For Israeli exits founded between 2007 and 2009 a founding age of 34.7 becomes a range of 36.7 to 38.4.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: In the United States the paper finds that founders who exit are older than the average entrepreneur, 46.7 against 41.9 for all employers. In Israel the pattern runs the other way. On the same timing adjustment the Israeli exited founder averages 37.6, below the Israeli all employer figure of 39.5 and far below the United States 46.7, a gap of 9.1 years. The same pattern holds across the 1,696 venture backed exits and the 531 above 100 million dollar exits, where restricting to larger or venture backed outcomes barely moves the Israeli figure. This is suggestive evidence that Israeli founders reach successful outcomes at younger ages than their United States counterparts, a real ecosystem difference.

papers.title: Cost of Experimentation and the Evolution of Venture Capital
papers.authorsFull: Michael Ewens, Ramana Nanda, Matthew Rhodes-Kropf
papers.comparisonNote: Every paper figure here is a descriptive statistic from the paper Table 1, computed across its full sample of United States venture backed Seed and Series A startups that raised a first round between 2002 and 2010. We recompute each statistic on the matching Israeli startups.
papers.abstract: We study how technological shocks to the cost of starting new businesses have led the venture capital model to adapt in fundamental ways over the prior decade. We both document and provide a framework to understand the changes in the investment strategy of venture capitalists in recent years, an increased prevalence of a spray and pray investment approach, where investors provide a little funding and limited governance to an increased number of startups that they are more likely to abandon, but where initial experiments significantly inform beliefs about the future potential of the venture. This adaptation and related entry by new financial intermediaries has led to a disproportionate rise in innovations where information on future prospects is revealed quickly and cheaply, and reduced the relative share of innovation in complex technologies where initial experiments cost more and reveal less.
keyFindings: After Amazon Web Services launched in 2006, the cost of starting a software company dropped, and first round funding for startups in cloud exposed sectors fell about 20 percent.
Investors responded with a spray and pray approach, smaller first checks into more startups, less hands on governance, and fewer board seats.
The teams they backed were younger and less experienced. The profile of an early stage bet became riskier rather than simply weaker.
These startups failed more often after the first round, so the downside grew. But the winners won bigger, with higher step ups in value and stronger exit returns on the capital they raised.
The net effect was higher variance early stage investing, more long shot bets rather than worse bets.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States venture capital backed startups that raised a first, early stage round between 2002 and 2010, sourced from the VentureSource database. Firm and founder characteristics are measured as of the first financing.

intro: The paper reports the capital a startup raised in its first venture round. We recompute the same figure on the first equity round of Israeli startups, in millions of United States dollars. Where the exact amount is missing we use the midpoint of the recorded amount range, which lifts the number of companies we can place from 364 to 650.

The Israeli median first round is 0.7 against 2.3 in the paper. The broad equity set is made up largely of small seed rounds under a million dollars, which sit well below the paper. The picture splits in two. Series A and later rounds have a median of 5.0, above the paper, while the seed heavy whole set sits far below it.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper takes the dollar size of the first venture round for each startup, in the period 2002 to 2010, and reports the average and median across firms.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We take the amount of the first equity round for each Israeli startup, in millions of US dollars, setting aside grants, debt and other non equity instruments. When the exact amount is not recorded we use the midpoint of the amount range the round was placed in. The two sub rows below split the set by round type and by whether an exact figure was on record.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The typical first round in the broad set is small, a median of 0.7 against 2.3 in the paper, because the set is dominated by seed rounds under a million dollars. The priced rounds tell the other half of the story. Series A and later rounds have a median of 5.0, above the paper figure. The paper number sits between the small Israeli seeds and the larger Israeli priced rounds.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli startups with any equity round, founded 2002-2010

detailRows.label: Series A and later
detailRows.population: First priced round, Series A or later

intro: The paper reports how old a startup is, in years, when it raises its first venture round. We recompute the same gap from the real founding date to the real first equity round date for Israeli startups.

At the median an Israeli startup raises a little faster than in the paper, about 11 months against 15. Half of Israeli equity backed startups raise within their first year, with a long tail that waited several years, which lifts the average above the median.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper measures the years between when a startup is founded and when it raises its first venture round, then reports the average and median across firms.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We measure the same gap in years from the recorded founding date to the date of the first equity round.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: At the median Israeli startups raise their first round about 4 months sooner than in the paper. The Israeli mean sits a little higher, pulled up by a tail of companies that waited several years before raising.

intro: The paper reports the average age of the founding team at the time of the first venture round. Because this is measured at first financing, later than legal founding and later than the first employer date the Azoulay paper uses, it should read older than a founding age.

Measured at that same point the Israeli founding team averages 38.5 against 40.3 in the paper. For companies whose first priced round is a Series A or later the Israeli figure rises to 41.0, just past the paper number. The remaining gap reflects when the age is measured more than a real difference in founder age.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper averages the ages of the founding team members and reports that team average as of the date of the first venture round.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: For each Israeli team we average the founder ages, then add the time from founding to the first equity round so the age is measured at first financing, the same point the paper uses. We then average across teams.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Measured at first financing the broad Israeli team averages 38.5, and the Series A and later set averages 41.0 against the paper figure of 40.3. The match is close for the comparable priced round set. The middle aged founding team that the paper documents in the United States is present in Israel as well.

detailRows.population: First priced round, Series A or later

intro: The paper reports the share of startups whose founding team includes at least one founder who had founded a company before. We recompute the same share on Israeli teams from prior company records.
A repeat founder appears on Israeli teams more often, 38 percent against 26 percent in the paper, on the same definition of a founder who had founded a company before.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper flags a team as serial when at least one founder had previously founded another company, then reports the share of teams that carry that flag.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We flag an Israeli team as serial when at least one founder has a recorded prior founded company, then take the share across teams in the same period.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Repeat founders are more common in the Israeli set, 38 percent against 26 percent, a higher share of teams that include at least one founder who had started a company before.

intro: The paper reports the share of funded startups that had gone out of business by the end of its observation window. We recompute the share of Israeli startups now recorded as closed or inactive.

The Israeli share for the 2002-2010 cohort is higher, 70 percent against 51 percent in the paper. Restricting to Series A and later rounds produces 76 percent, so the gap is not explained by lightly-funded seed companies alone. The past 10 years cohort shows a much lower share, but that window is still too recent for most of those companies to have closed.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper marks a startup as failed when it had gone out of business by the end of its observation window, and reports the share that failed.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We mark an Israeli startup as failed when its current status is closed or inactive, then take the share across the cohort. Our broad equity definition includes small single round companies that the paper sample does not always reach.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The Israeli failure share for the 2002-2010 cohort is 70 percent against 51 percent in the paper. The Series A and later subset reaches 76 percent, indicating the gap is not driven by small lightly-funded companies. The past 10 years cohort is lower at 29 percent, though that window is still too early to be directly comparable with the paper observation period.

intro: The paper reports the share of funded startups that went on to raise at least one more round. We recompute the share of Israeli startups with two or more equity rounds.
The two are very close, 55 percent in Israel against 57 percent in the paper. This is the tightest match in the table.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper reports the share of funded startups that received at least one follow on round after the first.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We take the share of Israeli startups in the cohort that have two or more equity rounds on record.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Follow on rates are almost identical, 55 percent against 57 percent. The chance that a first round is followed by another looks the same in the two markets.

intro: The paper reports the step up in value from the first round to the second, the second round valuation divided by the first. Valuation data is rarely reported for Israeli rounds, so we stand in the ratio of the second round amount to the first. It is a close cousin of the valuation step up rather than the same number.

On that basis the median step up is 1.67 in Israel against 1.63 in the paper, a close match. The Israeli mean runs well above its median, pulled up by a few cases where a small first round was followed by a much larger second round.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper takes the second round valuation divided by the first round valuation, the step up in value, and reports the average and median.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: Valuation data is rarely reported for Israeli rounds, so we use the ratio of the second round amount to the first round amount as a stand in for the value step up. It moves in the same direction as a valuation step up but is not the same measurement.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: On the amount stand in the median step up is 1.67 in Israel against 1.63 in the paper, which is close. The average runs higher, at 3.22 against 2.24, pulled up by a few cases where a small first round was followed by a much larger second round. Restricting to Series A to B priced rounds the median is 1.50, the closest comparison to the paper valuation step up.

intro: The paper reports the ratio of a startup exit value to the total capital it raised, for the startups that reached an exit. We recompute the same multiple on Israeli exits with a disclosed deal value.

At the median the multiple is close, 3.20 times in Israel against 3.52 times in the paper. The averages differ more, since a handful of very large outcomes move the mean, and only about forty percent of Israeli exits carry a disclosed value.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper divides the exit value of a startup by the total capital it raised, for the startups that reached an acquisition or public listing, and reports the average and median.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We divide the recorded exit value by the total capital raised for Israeli startups that exited with a disclosed deal value. Only about forty percent of exits carry a disclosed value, so this is a partial view.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The typical multiple is 3.20 times in Israel against 3.52 times in the paper. The broader cohorts are consistent, with all years giving a median of 2.49 across 550 exits and the past 10 years 2.47 across 326 exits. The median is stable, while the mean runs higher in all windows because a few very large outcomes pull it up. Only about forty percent of Israeli exits carry a disclosed value, so this remains a partial view.

papers.title: Lowering the Bar? External Conditions, Opportunity Costs, and High-Tech Start-Up Outcomes
papers.comparisonNote: Every paper figure here is a founder level descriptive statistic from the paper Table 1, computed across its sample of United States biotechnology and medical device founders, for companies founded between 2004 and 2012. We recompute each statistic on the matching Israeli healthcare founders. Founding team size is the average number of founders per company.
papers.abstract: We assess the heterogeneous impact of economic downturns on individuals decisions to bring high-technology ideas to the market in the form of new ventures. We thereby examine how worsening labor market conditions influence individuals opportunity costs of starting new ventures, the resulting composition of the entrepreneurial pool, and start-up performance outcomes. Using a rich data set of start-up founders in the biotechnology and medical device sectors, we find that an increase in the unemployment rate is associated with a substantial rise in the share of entrepreneurs who are most sensitive to worsening labor market conditions. Additionally, we find that start-ups founded by these entrepreneurs display lower financial and innovative performance than start-ups founded by entrepreneurs who are relatively insensitive to business cycles. Finally, we provide suggestive evidence that individuals heterogeneous response to worsening labor market conditions is a relevant factor in explaining the negative relationship between unemployment and start-up performance outcomes.
keyFindings: A rise in the state unemployment rate raises the share of founders most exposed to the labor market, PhDs and postdocs and people who had worked at small firms.
Start-ups founded by these more exposed entrepreneurs show lower financial and innovative performance than those founded by less exposed founders.
Students and small firm founders, the ones drawn in when labor markets weaken, have the poorest outcomes, which helps explain why start-ups founded in downturns tend to perform worse.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States biotechnology and medical device start-ups founded between 2004 and 2012, drawn from Crunchbase and enriched from LinkedIn, Bloomberg and patent records. Founder characteristics are measured at the time of founding.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli healthcare founders, companies founded 2004-2012

detailRows.label: Biotech and medical device
detailRows.population: Biotech, medical device and pharma founders

intro: The paper reports the share of founders who are women, among founders of biotechnology and medical device start-ups in the United States. We recompute the same share on the founders of Israeli healthcare companies founded in the same window.
Women are a similar share on both sides, 11 percent in Israel against 9 percent in the paper. The share holds across the biotech and medical device cut and rises to 18 percent in the past ten years.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper counts founders whose recorded gender is female and reports the share across all founders in its sample.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We take the founders of Israeli healthcare companies and report the share recorded as female, across founders with a known gender.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The female founder share is close on both sides, 11 percent in Israel against 9 percent in the paper. It rises to 18 percent for companies founded in the past ten years, so the recent Israeli cohort runs above the paper.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli healthcare founders, companies founded 2004-2012

detailRows.label: Biotech and medical device
detailRows.population: Biotech, medical device and pharma founders

intro: The paper reports the share of founders who had started a venture before. We recompute the share of Israeli healthcare founders with a recorded prior founded company, the same idea of a founder who had founded a company before.

Prior founders are far more common in Israel, 28 percent against 11 percent in the paper. The biotech and medical device cut is close at 27 percent, and the past ten years cohort is higher at 39 percent.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper flags a founder who had started another venture before the occupation it uses to build its employment categories, then reports the share of founders carrying that flag.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We flag an Israeli founder who has a recorded prior founded company, then report the share across founders of healthcare companies.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Repeat founders are much more common in the Israeli set, 28 percent against 11 percent in the paper. The biotech and medical device cut is close at 27 percent, and the past ten years cohort is higher again at 39 percent.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli healthcare founders, companies founded 2004-2012

detailRows.label: Biotech and medical device
detailRows.population: Biotech, medical device and pharma founders

intro: The paper reports the share of founders whose highest university degree is from a top-tier university. We recompute the same share on Israeli healthcare founders, counting the top schools in Israel.
A top school background is common on both sides and a little more common in Israel, 56 percent against 40 percent in the paper.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper counts founders whose highest university degree is from a university ranked among the top twenty worldwide in the 2016 Shanghai ranking.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We count founders whose highest degree is from one of the top Israeli universities. Israel has many institutions and only a handful are regarded as its top schools, so this stays a small set of universities.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Founders from top schools are a large share on both sides, 56 percent in Israel against 40 percent in the paper. The biotech and medical device cut is similar at 60 percent.

claims.ourPopulation: Israeli healthcare companies founded 2004-2012
intro: The paper reports the average number of founders per company, with the spread from the smallest to the largest team. We recompute the same on Israeli healthcare companies founded in the same window.

Israeli founding teams are a little larger, 2.1 founders against 1.7 in the paper, on a similar spread from one founder to a long tail of larger teams. The paper also breaks this out by sector, medical devices and biotechnology, which we match below.

explanations.title: How they measure
explanations.body: The paper identifies the founding team of each company and reports the average number of founders per company, with the minimum, median and maximum, for the full sample and for the medical device and biotechnology sectors.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We count the recorded founders of each Israeli healthcare company and report the same figures, matching the medical device and biotechnology sectors against the paper own breakdown.

explanations.title: What this means

papers.title: Founder Replacement and Startup Performance
papers.comparisonNote: Every paper figure here is a full sample firm level descriptive statistic from the paper Table 2, computed across its 11,929 United States venture backed startups founded between 1995 and 2008, with outcomes tracked to April 2017. We recompute each statistic on the matching Israeli startups that raised at least one equity round in the same window. The paper founder human capital measures are collected only for replaced founders, so they are not comparable to our all founders data and are left out.
papers.abstract: We provide causal evidence that venture capitalists (VCs) improve the performance of their portfolio companies by replacing founders. Augmenting a database of United States based, VC-backed startups founded from 1995 to 2008 with hand-collected information regarding turnover, we exploit shocks to the supply of outside executives via 14 state-level changes to the enforceability of employee non-compete agreements. Although naive regressions of startup performance on founder replacement would suggest a negative correlation, this may be due to selection as founders are likely to leave or be pushed out of poorly-performing startups. Indeed, instrumented regressions reverse the sign of this effect, suggesting that replacing founders instead improves the performance of venture-backed startups. Replacement helps more when founders are C-level executives or when they leave the startup after relinquishing their role. The evidence points to the replacement of founders as a specific mechanism by which VCs add value.
keyFindings: Naive comparisons make founder replacement look bad, because founders tend to leave or be pushed out of startups that are already doing poorly.
Using state changes in non-compete law as an instrument, the causal effect reverses. Replacing a founder raises the chance of a high quality exit such as an IPO or a strong acquisition.

Replacement matters most when the departing founder held a C-level role such as chief executive or chief financial officer, and when that founder leaves the company rather than staying on in a lesser role.
Founders are replaced more often after a new funding round and when investors hold more board seats.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States venture capital backed startups founded between 1995 and 2008, drawn from the VentureSource database, with outcomes tracked through April 2017. The firm statistics compared here are measured across the full sample of 11,929 companies.

intro: The paper reports the total capital a startup raised across all of its venture rounds. We recompute the same total on the equity rounds of Israeli startups founded in the same window, in millions of US dollars. The paper reports the full distribution, so we draw a box plot, with the whisker capped at the upper fence because a few very large raises would otherwise flatten the axis.

The averages are close, 45.5 in Israel against 37.6 in the paper, but both are lifted by a few very large raises. At the median, where outliers carry less weight, the Israeli total is 8.1 against 16.0 in the paper, so a typical Israeli startup raises about half as much in total.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We sum the amount of every equity round for each Israeli startup, in millions of United States dollars, setting aside grants, debt and other non equity instruments. When the exact amount is not recorded we use the midpoint of the amount range the round was placed in. Older rounds carry an exact figure less often, so this set is the startups with at least one amount on record.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Total capital looks similar on average, 45.5 against 37.6, but the average on both sides is pulled up by a handful of very large raises. The median is the steadier read, 8.1 in Israel against 16.0 in the paper, so the typical Israeli startup raises roughly half the lifetime capital of its United States counterpart.

papers.title: Are Early Stage Investors Biased Against Women?
papers.comparisonNote: The paper headline is the behaviour of investors on AngelList, which is specific to that platform and cannot be recomputed here. What we recompute is its descriptive contribution, the representation of women among founders from Table 1 and the full set of founder and startup variables from Table 2. Each Table 2 stat shows an All figure, the paper male and female pooled against all Israeli founders of known gender, and opens to a female and a male row. The unit is a founder, so the gender split adds up to the All figure. Each row carries its own note on how closely the two sides can be compared.
papers.abstract: We examine whether male investors are biased against female entrepreneurs. To do so, we use a proprietary dataset from AngelList covering fundraising startups. We find that female founders are less successful with male investors compared to observably similar male founders. In contrast, the same female founders are more successful than male founders with female investors. The results do not appear to be driven by differences across founder gender in startup quality, sector focus, or risk. Given that investors are predominately male, our results suggest that an increase in female investors is likely necessary to support an increase in female entrepreneurship.
keyFindings: Female founders draw less interest and funding from male investors than observably similar male founders, with the gap significant at the 1 percent level.
The same female founders do better with female investors, and the male led startups that male investors back do not outperform the female led ones they pass over.
Only about 21 percent of founders raising on AngelList are women, and 15.8 percent of founder chief executives, so much of the gender gap is present before investors get involved.
Male and female founders look broadly similar on observable human capital such as age, experience and education, so the gap in investor interest is not explained by those differences.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States startups raising capital on AngelList from late 2009 to 2017, with founder traits drawn from AngelList and LinkedIn profiles and gender assigned by an algorithm and manual review. Investor interest is measured through sharing, introduction requests and funding on the platform, which we cannot recompute. The biggest structural difference our recompute surfaces is team size. AngelList is a solo heavy early pipeline, a mean of 1.3 founders and 80 percent solo, while Israeli startups are team founded, a mean of 2.3 founders and 18 percent solo. Where the measures line up, age, experience and the failure rate, the two markets are close, and the near parity between female and male founders that the paper documents holds in Israel too.

papers.title: Funding Black High-Growth Startups
papers.authorsFull: Lisa D. Cook, Matt Marx, Emmanuel Yimfor
papers.comparisonNote: The paper compares Black founded startups against other startups in PitchBook. Black founders are a negligible share in Israel, so we set the Israeli ecosystem against the paper non Black column, the larger reference group. Each stat shows an All figure, the paper non Black mean against all Israeli startups or founders over the same window. The patent subset is the startups with a patent filed within five years of founding.
papers.abstract: We analyze determinants of access to venture capital for Black founders of high growth startups. We combine image and name processing algorithms with clerical review to identify race for over 100,000 startup founders at risk for venture funding. Black founders raise roughly one third as much venture capital in the five years after founding versus other startups formed in the same year, industry, and state. About one third of the gap is explained by differences in founding team size, shared work and educational networks between founders and investors, location in regions with less venture capital, and patenting. The funding gap is not statistically different from zero in later funding stages after Series B, suggesting that some investors initially hold but later amend incorrect beliefs about Black founded startups.
keyFindings: Black founded startups raise roughly one third as much venture capital in their first five years as otherwise similar startups formed in the same year, industry and state.

About one third of the gap is explained by observable differences, including smaller founding teams, fewer shared work and school ties to investors, location away from venture capital hubs and less patenting.
The funding gap fades in later stages after Series B, which the authors read as some investors holding and then correcting mistaken beliefs about Black founded startups.
The authors identify founder race for more than one hundred thousand founders using image and name processing with clerical review, drawn from PitchBook startups formed between 2000 and 2020.
papers.paperNote: The study covers United States startups in PitchBook formed between 2000 and 2020, with founder race identified from profile images and names through algorithms and clerical review. The headline is the venture funding gap for Black founders, which we cannot recompute in Israel, so what we take is the descriptive contribution, the founder and startup characteristics of the non Black reference group. Where the measures line up, founding vintage, early funding, public offerings and acquisitions, the two markets are close, while Israeli teams are larger, more often serial and far more patent intensive.

papers.title: State of High-Tech 2026
papers.authorsFull: Inbal Orpaz and Rachel Cooper Bar, Economics and Research Division, Israel Innovation Authority
papers.dataset: IVC, Crunchbase, Central Bureau of Statistics
papers.abstract: After two years of slowdown, Israeli high-tech returned to growth in 2025. Venture capital fundraising rose about 30 percent over 2024, exit value reached a record near 84 billion dollars across mergers, acquisitions and public listings, and Israel ranked as the fourth largest fundraising hub in the world and the first outside the United States. About 775 new startups were founded, the first rise after a decade of decline. High-tech reached a record 18.3 percent of GDP and contributed roughly half of the growth of the economy, with output up about 8.2 percent and a record 58 percent of total exports. The report also flags a concentration of fundraising in fewer and larger rounds, and a continued expansion of employment abroad.
keyFindings: Venture capital fundraising rose about 30 percent over 2024.
Exit value reached a record near 84 billion dollars across mergers, acquisitions and public listings.
Israel ranked the fourth largest fundraising hub in the world, the first outside the United States.
About 775 new startups were founded, the first rise after a decade of decline.
High-tech reached a record 18.3 percent of GDP and about half of the growth of the economy.
High-tech output grew about 8.2 percent and made up a record 58 percent of total exports.
papers.paperNote: An annual macro report on the Israeli high-tech sector from the Israel Innovation Authority, covering employment, companies and investors for the year 2025.

timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, figure on page 31 (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority counts new technology companies founded in Israel each year, the figure on page 31 of the report, sourced from the IVC database. The report marks its most recent years as a forecast. We use the actual figures it reports, not the forecast.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We count Israeli startups in our database by their recorded founding year over the same window. We use the same definition as the report, a startup with significant activity in Israel, including companies led by Israeli founders but incorporated abroad. We source them directly from the web, public registries and ongoing monitoring.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Both the report and our data show new company formation cooling over the past decade. The reported counts peak in the early 2010s and fall from there, while our recorded foundings rise into the early 2020s, because young companies keep surfacing in the record for years after they start, then drop off in the most recent, still incomplete years.

timeSeries.metric: Number of funding rounds by year
timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, Number and Scope of Funding Rounds figure (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority counts the venture funding rounds raised by Israeli companies each year, the line on the funding rounds figure, sourced from the IVC database. The report notes the count includes only rounds with a reported amount, so the true number of rounds each year is higher.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We count the equity funding rounds of Israeli startups by the year of the round, excluding grants, debt and other non-equity instruments. A round with no disclosed amount is sized at the median of disclosed rounds of the same type. The solid bar is the disclosed rounds and the lighter cap is the rounds we sized by estimate.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: The number of rounds climbs through the 2010s, peaks in 2021, then settles back. The report and our data trace the same path. Our deal count runs above the report in the most recent years, as recent rounds are still being recorded on both sides.

timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, Total Fundraising by Funding Round Size figure (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority sums the capital raised by Israeli companies each year, in billions of dollars, sourced from the IVC database. IVC counts the private funding rounds of Israeli and Israel-related high-tech companies, where Israel-related means a company led by Israeli founders even when it is incorporated abroad. It spans rounds from seed to late growth and estimates the size of rounds whose amount is not publicly disclosed.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We sum the amounts of those same equity rounds, in billions of dollars, by the year of the round. About half of the rounds report an exact amount. For a round with no amount we estimate it as the median amount of disclosed rounds of the same type, so a seed round takes the seed median and a Series A the Series A median. The solid part of each bar is the disclosed total and the lighter part is the estimated addition.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Capital builds through the 2010s, spikes in the 2021 boom, then recovers. With the undisclosed rounds estimated, our total tracks the report closely across the window, and most closely in the earlier years, where fundraising has finished being recorded. The size split tells the same story as the report. Dollars in small rounds under $10m stay flat and then fade, while rounds above $50m carry most of the growth, so the capital concentrates in a handful of large deals. The most recent years read a little above the report on our side, as recent rounds are still being recorded.

timeSeries.metric: AI share of fundraising by year
timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, Investments in Core AI Companies figure (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority shows the share of total annual investment that went to core AI companies, sourced from the IVC database, for 2022 to 2025. It defines core AI as companies building foundational AI capabilities, language models, infrastructure and enabling technologies such as computing infrastructure and chips, and not companies that merely add AI features to their products.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We take the same canonical Israeli startups and equity rounds and, for each year, divide the capital raised by core AI companies by the total capital raised. We read core AI from our taxonomy, the AI and Machine Learning subindustry plus the chip and cloud infrastructure companies whose work is AI focused, which mirrors the report scope and leaves out companies that merely add AI features. About half of the rounds report an exact amount. For a round with no amount we use the median amount of disclosed rounds of the same type, so a seed round takes the seed median and a Series A the Series A median.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: AI is taking a fast growing share of Israeli fundraising. Both the report and our data climb steeply year after year. Our line follows the same trajectory at a lower level, so the trend matches even though the levels do not. The direction and the steep climb are the shared signal rather than the exact share.

timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, Number and Value of Exits figure (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority counts the exits of Israeli companies each year, sourced from the IVC database. An exit is a merger or acquisition where the Israeli company is bought, or an initial public offering. The report counts a deal in the year it is announced, so the large deals announced in 2025 and approved in 2026 still count in 2025.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We count the same events for Israeli startups in our database, an acquisition where the Israeli company is the one acquired plus a public offering that priced, by the year the deal was announced. We keep only deals that actually went ahead, leaving out announcements that were later called off or were only rumored.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Both lines trace the same path, a climb through the 2010s and a sharp peak in the 2021 boom. The counts differ in level, but the shape of the cycle is the shared signal.

timeSeries.source: State of High-Tech 2026, Number and Value of Exits figure (IVC data).

explanations.title: How the report measures
explanations.body: The Israel Innovation Authority sums the value of Israeli exits each year, in billions of dollars, sourced from the IVC database. It splits the value into mergers and acquisitions and initial public offerings, the two sets of bars on the Number and Value of Exits figure. It counts the value in the year the deal is announced and leaves the three large 2025 deals, Wiz, CyberArk and Armis, out of the bars, showing them separately because together they reach about 84 billion dollars and would dwarf every other year.

explanations.title: How we measure
explanations.body: We sum the disclosed value of the same events for Israeli startups in our database, the acquisitions where the Israeli company is bought plus the capital raised in IPOs that priced, by the year the deal was announced. Many acquisitions never disclose a price, and like the report we let those add no value rather than estimate them. We count each deal once and, to match the report, leave the 2025 Wiz and Armis deals out, which is most of the difference in the latest year. The two segments, mergers and acquisitions and IPOs, add up to the total.

explanations.title: What this means
explanations.body: Exit value swings with a few large deals, so the line is spikier than the deal count. Our disclosed total tracks the report closely across the window, with merger and acquisition value carrying most of the picture and IPO value small outside the 2021 boom. Where the largest deals are undisclosed our total sits a little below the report, since neither side estimates a price for them. We hold the 2025 mega-deals aside here, the same as the report.
